constructive_proposals: yielding to nature within legal bounds
applying medicinal cures for the soul
redirecting love toward more worthy objects
avoiding presence of the beloved to distract the mind
admonishment and expostulation about the misery of inordinate love
vilifying the character of the beloved through correction
assigning arduous duties to distract thoughts
confession and frequenting shrines of the Saints, especially the Blessed Virgin

scapegoat.name: the devil (via witches)
attributes: stirs up hatred between married people
targets noble, rich men as primary victims
scapegoat.justification: The text attributes the root cause of widespread immoral behavior — especially adultery among the powerful — to the devil's influence acting through witches. Witches are portrayed not merely as practitioners but as agents of the devil who manipulate minds to inordinate love or hatred, making them the central deconstructed and condemned force behind societal decay.

moral_justification: Inordinate lust is framed as a path to eternal damnation — losing Heaven and facing eternal fire. The text presents divine law and spiritual salvation as the only true remedy, contrasting earthly pleasure with everlasting punishment. Moral order is upheld through religious discipline, confession, and virtue.
summary: The passage deconstructs inordinate love and adultery as societal ills rooted in devilish witchcraft, particularly targeting powerful men, while offering a mix of physical, moral, legal, and spiritual remedies grounded in Christian doctrine to restore virtue and avoid eternal damnation.
